assemblies.name: Z-Front Axis Bench Build
assemblies.description: Assembles the Z-front axis module from individual parts. Z-Front uses the same plastic piece type as Y-Right. No endstop is attached to Z axes. The long M6x30 frame-mount bolt on the idler is left loose at bench build. Produces a complete Z-front axis module ready for mounting on the front frame face.
assemblies.learningFocus: Z-Front uses the same build procedure as Y-Right (same plastic piece type). No endstop. The spacer pieces go between the carriage and the half-carriage during rod threading. The second belt peg is left loose — it is tensioned after the axis is mounted and sized to the front frame face.

subassemblies.name: Z-Front Carriage Assembly
subassemblies.description: Same carriage build as Y-Right (same piece type). Seat bearings, align belt holes, bolt halves. Run shake and rod-slide tests.
subassemblies.milestoneMessage: Z-front carriage assembled and bearing fit verified.

subassemblies.name: Z-Front Idler Assembly
subassemblies.description: Same idler build as Y-Right. Long M6x30 frame-mount bolt placed loosely — it will size to the front frame face bottom hole. No bearing in the middle of Z idler (Z uses the same idler piece as Y-Right).
subassemblies.milestoneMessage: Z-front idler assembled. Frame-mount bolt left loose.

subassemblies.name: Z-Front Motor Holder Assembly
subassemblies.description: Same motor holder build as Y-Right. Belt in channel, motor attached. All 8 motor-piece bolts will be tightened before frame mounting (they become inaccessible after).

subassemblies.name: Z-Front Axis Rod Assembly
subassemblies.description: Threads rods through idler, carriage (with spacers z2_spacer and z2_spacer_2 between carriage and half-carriage), and motor holder. Checks rods flush at idler.
subassemblies.milestoneMessage: Z-front guide rods threaded with spacers in place.

subassemblies.name: Z-Front Belt Threading
subassemblies.description: Routes belt, locks first peg, places second peg loosely. No endstop for Z axes. Label axis Z-Front.
subassemblies.milestoneMessage: Z-front axis complete: belt routed, first peg locked, axis labeled and QC checked.

subassemblies.name: Z Front Complete Axis Unit
subassemblies.description: The fully assembled z front axis unit, ready for staging. Contains all bench-built components: carriage, idler, motor holder, rods, and belt.

parts.name: Z-Front Leadscrew Idler
parts.function: Supports the front Z-axis leadscrew at the bottom of the frame.

parts.name: Z-Front Stepper Motor
parts.function: Drives the front Z-axis leadscrew for vertical X-gantry motion.

parts.name: Z-Front Motor Mount
parts.function: Mounts the Z-front stepper motor to the top of the frame.

parts.name: Z-Front Carriage
parts.function: Rides on the front Z column and carries the X-axis gantry end.

parts.name: Z-Front Upper Spacer
parts.function: Positions the front Z leadscrew bearing at the correct height.

parts.name: Z-Front Lower Spacer
parts.function: Positions the front Z leadscrew idler at the bottom of the frame.

steps.name: Lay out axis pieces
steps.instructionText: Gather and lay out all axis pieces: two carriage half-pieces, two idler half-pieces, two motor holder half-pieces, the stepper motor, both guide rods, and all hardware (M3x25 screws, M6x18 and M6x30 bolts, nuts).

steps.name: Clean excess plastic from holes
steps.instructionText: Use a box cutter or cordless power drill to clear excess plastic from all semi-circle rod holes in the carriage, idler, and motor holder half-pieces. Holes must be smooth so rods insert freely and belts do not rub.

steps.name: QC: verify all plastic pieces are clean
steps.instructionText: Verify: each carriage half has 4 clean semi-circles, each motor holder half has 6, each idler half has 8. All M6 bolt holes and M3 screw holes are clear of extra plastic.

steps.name: Place 4 bearings in carriage half
steps.instructionText: Place four linear bearings into one carriage half-piece with the flanges facing outward (away from each other).

steps.name: Shake test: bearings must not rattle

guidance.instructionText: Hold both carriage halves firmly together and shake the assembly.
guidance.whyItMattersText: Compress the two halves by hand until there is no gap. Shake side-to-side and up-down for 2–3 seconds. No tools needed — this is a hand-compression test only. Tip: grip a completed carriage from another axis as a tactile reference for correct feel.

validation.successCriteria: No audible rattle or tactile movement from the bearings.
validation.failureCriteria: Bearings click, rattle, or shift inside the halves when shaken.

feedback.successMessage: Bearings are snug. Proceed to the rod slide test.
feedback.failureMessage: Bearing fit is too loose. Swap to a slightly larger LM8UU or try the next batch of bearings. Re-test before closing the carriage.

steps.name: Rod slide test: slight resistance, not too free

guidance.instructionText: Insert a smooth guide rod through the carriage and hold it so the rod is vertical.
guidance.whyItMattersText: The rod should slide or drop through as you hold the carriage, but there should be a little resistance — it's good if you have to shake the carriage for the rod to slide through. Motors pull 20 lbs of belt force on this carriage, so it should not slip through too fast with no resistance.

validation.successCriteria: Rod slides or drops through with slight resistance. Shaking the carriage helps it move through.
validation.failureCriteria: Rod slides too quickly with zero resistance (too free), OR rod jams and requires force to push (too tight).

feedback.successMessage: Resistance feels correct. Both tests pass — proceed to close the carriage halves.
feedback.failureMessage: Too free: wrap each bearing in one layer of electrical tape to tighten the fit and retest. Too tight: check that bearings are fully seated and carriage halves are not warped.

steps.name: Bolt carriage halves together
steps.instructionText: Secure both halves with M6x18 bolts on top and M6x30 bolts on bottom. Critical alignment: the small ribbed belt hole must sit directly beside the large smooth belt hole. Wrong alignment mismatches the peg holes.

steps.name: Insert M6x18 bolt into idler half
steps.instructionText: Begin assembling the idler: place an M6x18 bolt through one idler half-piece from the outside.

steps.name: Stack two flanged bearings onto bolt
steps.instructionText: Insert two bearing pieces one on top of the other onto the bolt, flanges facing outward (away from each other).

steps.name: Align idler rod holes before closing
steps.instructionText: Hold the second idler half against the first and confirm the circular rod holes on both halves line up correctly before pressing them together.

steps.name: Bolt idler halves and secure bearing
steps.instructionText: Secure the idler with an M6x18 bolt through the top and one M6x30 bolt in the belt-side hole. Tighten until firm.

steps.name: Mount pulley on motor shaft with spacer
steps.instructionText: Rest the thin spacer on the motor housing, then seat the pulley on the shaft above it. Tighten the first set screw with an Allen key — the spacer keeps the pulley off the housing.

steps.name: Lock pulley set screw until it pops
steps.instructionText: Remove the spacer. Tighten the second set screw as firmly as possible, pressing near the Allen key midpoint. A soft "pop" means fully seated. Whether or not you hear it, tighten as hard as you can.

steps.name: Load motor holder half with nuts and M6x30 bolt
steps.instructionText: Orient the motor holder half with the rectangular belt channel facing you. Insert one M6x30 bolt and three nuts into the holes now — the motor will cover them and block access later.

steps.name: Insert belt in motor holder channel
steps.instructionText: Insert the belt into the channel in the motor holder half-piece with the toothed side facing inward toward where the pulley will sit.

steps.name: Close motor holder halves together
steps.instructionText: Press the two motor holder halves together with the belt seated in the channel.

steps.name: Confirm belt runs smoothly on pulley
steps.instructionText: Hold the motor in position against the holder and give the belt a gentle pull — it should slide along the motor pulley. If it does not, reseat the belt in the channel.

steps.name: Attach motor with 4 M3x25 screws
steps.instructionText: Insert the first M3x25 motor screw and tighten 3/4 of the way. Insert the remaining three screws and tighten each the same way — leave slightly loose so the motor retains flex for belt tensioning later.

steps.name: Insert M6x18 bolts into motor holder
steps.instructionText: Insert M6x18 bolts into the three remaining motor holder holes and tighten each into the nuts with an electric drill.

steps.name: Dangle test: motor holder hangs securely
steps.instructionText: Hold the complete motor assembly by one of its bolts. It should hang without wobble or separation. This confirms bolts are fully seated before rod insertion.

steps.name: Insert rods into idler, ends flush
steps.instructionText: Insert both guide rods into the completed idler piece. Rod ends must be flush against the bottom of the idler. If rods are hard to insert, loosen the idler bolts, slide rods in, then re-tighten.

steps.name: Tighten short idler bolts onto rods
steps.instructionText: Tighten the two shorter idler bolts with an electric drill to grip the rods firmly. Leave the longer bolt loose — it will be used to attach the axis to the frame.

steps.name: Slide carriage onto rods
steps.instructionText: Slide the completed carriage onto the rods with the long bolt ends closer to the idler. This orientation maximizes the travel distance.

steps.name: Push motor holder onto rods
steps.instructionText: Push the motor holder piece onto the rods so the motor faces the carriage bolt heads. Loosen bolts if needed to slide on, then re-tighten.

steps.name: QC: rods flush, carriage slides freely
steps.instructionText: Check that rod ends are still flush at the idler bottom. Slide the carriage the full rod length — it should move with only slight bearing resistance.

steps.name: Thread belt through large carriage hole
steps.instructionText: Inspect the carriage: one belt hole is large and smooth, the other small and ribbed. Insert one belt end through the large smooth hole with the toothed side facing inward.

steps.name: Route belt around idler bearing
steps.instructionText: Feed the belt around the bearing inside the idler. Tip: curve the belt slightly in the direction of travel first — it navigates around the bearing more easily.

steps.name: Thread belt back through small carriage hole
steps.instructionText: Once the belt is around the idler bearing, pull it back through the smaller ribbed carriage hole.

steps.name: Confirm belt peg orientation before locking
steps.instructionText: Thread the belt end through a belt peg. Confirm: the foot of the peg faces away from the toothed side of the belt and away from the axis center. Wrong orientation prevents the peg from locking correctly.

steps.name: Lock first belt peg
steps.instructionText: Insert the belt end approximately 3/4 inch into the small ribbed belt hole, then simultaneously insert the peg into the same hole and press firmly until it locks.

steps.name: Insert second peg loosely
steps.instructionText: Insert the second peg directly across from the first. Hand-tighten the belt by pulling it through the first peg as much as you can easily. Leave the second peg loose — it will be fully tensioned after mounting to the frame.

steps.name: Travel test: carriage moves smoothly with belt
steps.instructionText: Slide the carriage along the full rod length with the belt installed. It should move smoothly without binding. If it binds, check the belt is not twisted and both carriage holes are correctly threaded.

steps.name: Label completed Z-Front Axis and set aside
steps.instructionText: Use masking tape and a marker to label this assembly as "Z-Front Axis". Set it aside in a safe place. It is complete and ready for frame mounting.

steps.name: Stage completed Z-Front axis on bench
steps.instructionText: Z-Front axis assembly is complete. Place the assembled axis unit at the staging position on your bench. It will be mounted to the front face of the frame.

steps.name: Z-front axis bench QC and label
steps.instructionText: QC checklist: belt smooth along full rod length; rods flush with idler bottom; idler and carriage bolt heads on same side; toothed side of belt inward. Label axis Z-Front. Set aside for frame mounting.

hints.title: Four-point QC and label Z-Front
hints.message: Same four-point QC as Y axes. Label Z-Front. Set aside — it mounts on the front frame face.
